title: Algebraic Kolmogorov--Arnold representation theorem for quantum measurement
abstract: We establish an operational framework connecting the classical Kolmogorov--Arnold (KA) representation theorem to quantum information theory. By introducing and proving an algebraic, bounded-degree polynomial version of the theorem, we demonstrate that any target physical property of an unentangled multi-qubit product state can be exactly decomposed using a finite, fixed set of local <<inner>> observables and a shallow architecture of univariate polynomials.   We further analyze the stability of this Quantum Kolmogorov--Arnold (QKA) representation under adversarial perturbations. In stark contrast to the pathological instabilities and severe reparameterization sensitivities inherent to the classical Kolmogorov--Arnold representation theorem, our algebraic quantum framework exhibits remarkable resilience. We prove that the representation remains stable against bounded physical perturbations acting on the inner measurement operators, and show via the Heisenberg picture that it is inherently immune to adversarial quantum channel attacks acting on the input states.
summary: The paper develops the Quantum Kolmogorov–Arnold (QKA) representation theorem, showing that measuring properties of unentangled multi-qubit states can be reduced to a sum involving local measurements and univariate polynomials. Crucially, this algebraic framework is proven to be stable against both physical noise and adversarial quantum attacks.
main_problem: The paper establishes an operational framework connecting the classical Kolmogorov–Arnold (KA) representation theorem to quantum information theory by formulating a Quantum Kolmogorov–Arnold (QKA) representation for expectation values of observables.
main_result: It proves that the expected value of a general observable on unentangled multi-qubit product states can be exactly decomposed using a finite set of local inner observables and shallow univariate polynomials, exhibiting stability against perturbations and quantum attacks.
method: The method adapts the polynomial version of the classical KA theorem to the trace structure $	ext{tr}(Tho_{	ext{in}})$, utilizing algebraic techniques to ensure a fixed, bounded representation size.
model_or_system: The system analyzed is an unentangled multi-qubit product state ($ho_{	ext{in}} =  igotimes_{i=1}^n ho_i$) acting on $n$ qubits. The target property is the expectation value of a general observable $T \in O(\mathbb{C}^{2^n})$.
key_observables: The expected value $	ext{tr}(Tho_{	ext{in}})$, local inner observables $A_{j,k}$, and outer univariate polynomials $g_j$.
important_parameters: Number of qubits ($n$), number of required inner observables ($m \ge \lceil 4n-1 ceil$), and the perturbation bound $\epsilon$ for stability analysis.
main_assumptions: The primary results critically assume that the input state $ho_{	ext{in}}$ is a product state (unentangled). Generalization to entangled states or full unitary dynamics faces severe mathematical barriers.
figures_summary: Not specified in the notes, but the structure implies theorems demonstrating the decomposition and stability proofs.
paper_structure: The paper first reviews the classical KA theorem for polynomials, then introduces Theorem 2.1 (QKA) for static state properties $	ext{tr}(Tho_{	ext{in}})$, followed by analyses of its robustness: Theorem 3.1 proves stability under physical perturbations, and Theorem 3.2 extends this immunity to adversarial quantum channel attacks via the Heisenberg picture.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful mathematical tool for characterizing complex expectation values in simple product states, offering insights into how classical decomposition theorems can be rigorously adapted to quantum measurement theory while maintaining stability guarantees.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
